content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vitam_p126_l7"> , <gloss>
							lives</gloss>: the plural would rarely be used in Latin. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bona_p126_l8"> , <gloss>
							estates</gloss> (landed property); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortunas_p126_l8">
							, <gloss>goods</gloss> (personal property). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nascendi_condicio_p126_l16">
							, <gloss>the lot of birth</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illum_p126_l17">:
							Romulus, who, after his death, was deified and identified with the
							Sabine god of war, Quirinus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbi_p127_l4">,
							etc.: dat. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">subjectos</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_p127_l6">
							(plun), <gloss>I. . . have also</gloss>, etc.: § <ref n="AG 298">
								298</ref>, b (195, e); B. 248, I G. 310; H. 508, 3 (451, 3); H-B.
							270, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eorum_p127_l7">,
							i.e. of the swords. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrata_p127_l8">
							, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patefacta_p127_l8">
							, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comperta_p127_l8">:
							the anticlimax is only apparent, for <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								comperta</foreign> expresses the most difficult as well as the most
							important of the three acts. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vobis_p127_l9">:
							opposed to in <foreign xml:lang="lat">senatu</foreign> (1.8). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="investigata_p127_l10">
							, <gloss>traced out</gloss> (observe the figure). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exspectatis_p127_l11">
							, <gloss>are waiting to hear</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_p127_l12"> , <gloss>ever
							since</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possemus_p127_l16">:
							§ <ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, b (334, b); B. 300,2; G.467; cf. H. 642,
							3 (523, ii, i, N.). <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_eiciebam_p127_l16">:
							notice the tense (<gloss>at the time I was engaged in driving out</gloss>,
							etc., also <foreign xml:lang="lat">volebam</foreign>, below), as
							compared with <foreign xml:lang="lat">erupit</foreign> (<gloss>burst
							forth</gloss>, once for all). Notice also the difference in mood (<gloss>at
							the time</gloss>, etc.), compared with <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum
							reliquisset</foreign> in ll.13, 14 (not referring to time at all, but to
							circumstance: <gloss>having left behind</gloss>, etc.): see § <ref n="AG 545">545</ref>,546 (323, 325. a); B. 288, I; G. 580, 585; H.
							600, 601 (521, i, ii); H.-B. 550, a and N.1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiam_p127_l17">:
							see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl108.27">p. 108, l. 27</ref>, and note. <pb n="392"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p127_l18">,
							sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">invidia</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_exierit_p127_l18">:
							§ <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 539; H. 588, ii
							(516, ii); H.-B. 535, 2, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="restitissent_p127_l20">:
							in direct disc. this would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">restiterint</foreign>
							(fut. perf.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoniam_faceret_p127_l25">
							, <gloss>because</gloss> (as I thought), etc.: hence the subj. rather
							than <foreign xml:lang="lat">faciebat</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3, N. (34 i, d, R.); G. 541; H. 588, ii (516,
							ii); H.-B. 535, 2, a, N.2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fidem_faceret_p127_l26">
							, <gloss>gain credence</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oratio_p127_l26"> , <gloss>
							argument</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_comprehenderem_p127_l27">
							, <gloss>get hold of the matter</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_provideretis_p127_l27">:
							purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_videretis_p127_l28">:
							subj. of integral part (otherwise it would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								videbitis</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Allobrogum_p127_l29">:
							the Allobroges were a Gallic nation between the Rhone and the Alps (in
							the modern <foreign xml:lang="lat">Dauphine</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">Savoy</foreign>); subdued <date when="-0121">B.C.
								121</date>, and united with the province <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							Narbonensis</foreign>. They were restless under their new masters (see
							sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.22">22</ref>), and inclined to take up
							with Catiline's movement. Their ambassadors had come to complain of
							certain exactions of their provincial governor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="belli_p127_l29">,
							i.e. when out of the range of the Roman jurisdiction; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tumultus_p127_l29">
							, <gloss>rebellion</gloss>, i.e. when nearer home. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lentulo_p127_l30">,
							see Introd., p. 126: he had been consul <date when="-0071">B.C. 71</date>,
							but had been expelled from the Senate the next year, with sixty-three
							others, on account of his character, and he now held the praetorship
							with the view of beginning the career of office over again. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="manifesto_deprehenderetur_p128_l5">
							, <gloss>taken in the act</gloss>: the words apply strictly to the
							criminals themselves. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetores_p128_l7">:
							although the regular duties of the praetors were judicial, yet they
							possessed the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>, and in virtue
							of this could command troops in the absence of the consuls, or under
							their authority. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_sentirent_p128_l9">
							(subj. of characteristic), <gloss>as men who</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pontem_Mulvium_p128_l11">:
							the bridge over the Tiber, about two miles above the city, by which the
							principal roads (the Flaminian and Cassian) led into north Italy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inter_eos_p128_l13">,
							i.e. between the two divisions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praefectura_p128_l15">:
							the title given to the politically lowest class of Italian towns, which
							had lost their independence; cf. Vocab. under <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								colonia</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipium</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Reatina_p128_l15">:
							Reate was a very ancient town of the Sabines, about forty miles
							northeast of Rome. Cicero was the <foreign xml:lang="lat">patronus</foreign>
							of Reate, that is, acted as its attorney and legal counsel; which
							accounts for his having this body-guard of young men from that place.
							Besides, these simple mountaineers still retained something of the old
							Italian virtues, and therefore were well fitted for this service. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praesidio_p129_l1">:
							dat. of service. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tertia_exacta_p129_l2">,
							about 3 A.M.: the night, from sunset to sunrise, was divided by the
							Romans into four <foreign xml:lang="lat">vigiliae</foreign> of equal
							length. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magno_comitatu_p129_l3">:
							abl. of accomp.; § <ref n="AG 413">413</ref>, a (248, a, N.); cf. B.
							222, I ; G. 392, R.1; H. 474, 2 (419, I 1); cf. H.-B. 422, i. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_p129_l5">: the
							occasion of the attack. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignorabatur_p129_l6">,
							etc. Though the Allobroges had played the conspirators false, and knew
							that the consul had his plans ready, they did not know what these plans
							were, and therefore were as much taken by surprise as Volturcius
							himself. Even the troops would appear not to have known what special
							enterprise they were engaged in. <milestone n="129.11" unit="pg_l"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="machinatorem_p129_l6">:
							Gabinius had been the go-between in this case; he and Statilius were to
							burn the city (<ref>Sall. Cat. 43, 44</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="venit_p129_l14">:
							of course he had been summoned like the others. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeter_p129_l15">,
							etc., since Lentulus was notoriously lazy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viris_p129_l16">,
							dat. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">placeret</foreign>, which has for
							subject <foreign xml:lang="lat">litteras</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">aperiri</foreign>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deferrem_p129_l18">,
							integral part of <foreign xml:lang="lat">aperiri</foreign>; otherwise it
							would probably be defers; see § <ref n="AG 551">551</ref>, c (327, a);
							cf. 'B. 291, I ; G. 574; H. 605, 2 (520); H-B. 571,507, 4, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_facturum_p129_l20">
							governs the result clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_deferrem_p129_l20">:
							we may translate, <gloss>I said I would not fail to lay before the
								public council a matter touching the public danger before it had
								been
								tampered with</gloss> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integram_p129_l20">
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_si_p129_l22">
							, <gloss>for if you see</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reperta_essent_p129_l23">:
							in dir. disc. this would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">reperta erunt</foreign>
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_esset_p129_l27">
							, <gloss>whatever weapons there might be</gloss>. <milestone n="129.30" unit="pg_l"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="introduxi_p129_l27">,
							sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">in senatum</foreign>. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fidem publicam</foreign>, <gloss>assurance of
								safity</gloss>: he was to be used as state's evidence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sciret_p129_l31">:
							subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servorum_p130_l3">:
							the recollection of the terrible servile insurrections in Sicily, and
							especially that of Spartacus in Italy, less than ten years before, would
							make this shock and terrify Cicero's hearers beyond measure. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_uteretur_p130_l3">:
							§ <ref n="AG 563">563</ref> (331); B. 295,4; G. 546; H. 565 (498, i);
							H.-B. 502, 31 a; obj. of the verb of commanding implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">mandata</foreign>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_p130_l4">: in a
							sort of apposition with at . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								accederet</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cam_incendissent_p130_l5">:
							subj. because integral part of at <foreign xml:lang="lat">praesto
								esset</foreign>; otherwise it would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							incenderimus</foreign> (fut. perf.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_p130_l6">: § <ref n="AG 583">583</ref> (336, b); B. 314, 3; G. 628, R.; H. 643,4
							(524, 2); H.-B. 535, I, d. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_mitterent_p130_l12">:
							purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equitatum_p130_l12">:
							the Roman cavalry was at this time chiefly composed of Gallic and other
							auxiliaries. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sibi_p130_l13"> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="copias_p130_l13">)
							refers to the conspirators; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sibi_p130_l13_2"> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="confirmasse_p130_l13">)
							to the envoys: § <ref n="AG 300">300</ref>, I and 2 (I 96, a, I and
							2); B. 244, i, ii; H.-B. 262, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defuturas_p130_l13">
							[<foreign xml:lang="lat">esse</foreign>] depends on the idea of <gloss>
								saying</gloss> implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">praescriptum</foreign>
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fatis_p130_l14">:
							the books bought by Tarquinius Superbus of the Cumaean Sibyl. They were
							kept in charge of a board, <foreign xml:lang="lat">collegium</foreign>,
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">quindecemviri sacris faciundis</foreign>, and
							consulted in cases of great public emergency (cf. <ref n="Verg. A. 6.71">Aeneid 6. 71</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haruspicum_p130_l14">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">haruspices</foreign> were Etruscan soothsayers, who
							interpreted the will of the gods, chiefly from the entrails of animals
							sacrificed. They were a private class, of low standing, and are not to
							be confounded with the augurs, who were a board of Roman noblemen, of
							high rank, who interpreted the auspices according to the native Roman
							rules, chiefly by the flight of birds, by lightning, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cinnam_p130_l16">,
							etc.: L. Cornelius Cinna was colleague of Marius, and ruled Rome after
							his death, <date when="-0086">B.C. 86</date>. L. Cornelius Sulla ruled Rome <date from="-0082" to="-0079">
								<date when="-0082">B.C. 82</date>-79</date> (see sect. 24). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virginam_p130_l19">:
							the Vestal Virgins, six in number, maidens of high rank, consecrated to
							chastity and the service of Vesta. They were peculiarly sacred, and were
							highly privileged. Violation of their vow of chastity was <foreign xml:lang="lat">incestus</foreign>, and was regarded as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">prodigium</foreign> of very bad omen. Of the incident
							referred to here nothing further is known. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Capitoli_p130_l19">:
							the temple of Jupiter Capitolinus (see "Plunder of Syracuse," sect. 15)
							was burned during the rule of the Marian faction, <date when="-0083">B.C.
								83</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Saturnalibas_p130_l21">:
							a very ancient festival in honor of Saturn, the god of seed sowing,
							celebrated Dec.19. During this festival every serious business was
							suspended; and it was so complete a holiday that slaves feasted at the
							same tables with their masters. No better opportunity could be found for
							the outbreak of an insurrection than this season of unrestrained
							jollification. <milestone n="130.24" unit="pg_l"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabellas_p130_l21">
							, <gloss>
								tablets</gloss> of wood: wax was spread on the inside, and on this
							the writing was scratched with a <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								stilus</foreign>. When used for letters, the tablets were tied about
							with a linen thread, <foreign xml:lang="lat">linum</foreign>, and
							sealed. See Fig. 35 (from a Pompeian wall-painting) and Fig. 44. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsias_mana_p130_l27">:
							the ambassadors had made sure to get all the conspirators committed in
							writing except Cassius, who alone had the sagacity to keep out of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatai_p130_l27">:
							the Gallic tribes were governed by an aristocracy, having a council or
							senate as its mouthpiece. etc.: in direct disc., <foreign xml:lang="lat">faciam
								quae vestris legatis confirmavi</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_illi_p130_l29">,
							etc.: in the direct form, - <foreign xml:lang="lat">vos facite quae
								sibi
								vestri legati receperunt</foreign> Note the change of pronouns as
							well as of moods and tenses. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sibi_recepissent_p130_l29">
							, <gloss>had taken upon themselves</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_respondisset_p130_l30">
							: <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign> concessive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p130_l30">,
							i.e. in spite of the strong evidence against him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_vero_p131_l5">,
							etc., i.e. you may well recognize it: it is, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="avi_tui_p131_l6">:
							Cornelius Lentulus, cos. <date when="-0162">B.C. 162</date>. He was <foreign xml:lang="lat">princeps senatus</foreign>, that is, designated by
							the censors as first man of the Senate: an honorary office, held
							ordinarily by patricians. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="debuit_p131_l8"> , <gloss>ought
							to have recalled</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, a (288, a); B.
							270, 2; G. 254, R.1; H. 618, 2 (537, I) H.-B. 582, 31 a, and footnote.
							(The joining of such opposites as <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								mata</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">revocare</foreign> is
							called oxymoron, or paradox.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eadem_ratione_p131_l9">
							, <gloss>to
								the same purport</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_vellet_p131_l10">:
							§ <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 2 (341, c); Cf. B. 323; G. 663, 2, b; H.-B.
							536, a (direct, <foreign xml:lang="lat">si vis</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="feci_potestatem_p131_l10">
							, <gloss>I gave him leave</gloss>. <pb n="396"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihilne_p131_l16">:
							equiv. to <foreign xml:lang="lat">nonne aliquid</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_p132_l1"> , <gloss>
							is</gloss>: imperf. by seq. of tenses; § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, d
							(287, d); H. 549 (495, v); H.-B. 482, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qais_sim_p132_l11">,
							etc.: this letter is given with slight variations by Sallust, Cat. 44. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qaem_locam_p132_l12">,
							etc., <gloss>how far you have gone</gloss> (alluding to the fact that he
							was thoroughly compromised). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infimoram_p132_l14">,
							i.e. slaves; see note, <ref n="pl130.3">p. 130, l. 3</ref>, above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p132_l17"> , <gloss>the
							following</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, b (102, b); B. 246, 2;
							G. 307, 3; H. 507(450, 3). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="furtim_p132_l21"> , <gloss>
							stealthily</gloss> ("like thieves"); so English <gloss>stealth</gloss>
							from <gloss>
								steal</gloss>. <milestone n="132.23" unit="pg_l"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatum_consului_p132_l21">:
							deliberative assemblies in ancient times were under the control of the
							presiding officer, and members could not speak or introduce business
							except when called upon by him. He laid a subject before them (<foreign xml:lang="lat">consulere senatum, referre ad senatum</foreign>), and
							asked their opinions individually, in a definite order, usually
							according to their rank or dignity. In the case of a general question he
							was said <foreign xml:lang="lat">referre (consulere) de
								summa re publica</foreign>. The form would be, <foreign xml:lang="lat">dic, C. Juli, sententiam</foreign>. (See Introd., p.
							lvii.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_principibus_p132_l24">
							, <gloss>the
								leading men</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sententiae_p132_l25">:
							the views of the individual Senators (see note on l.23, above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perscriptum_p132_l27">:
							the opinions (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sententiae</foreign>) of the
							Senators (given as just described) merely determined the substance of
							the ordinance, which was afterwards written out in regular form by the
							secretaries in the presence of some of its advocates and under the
							direction of the presiding officer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Flaccas_p132_l31">:
							see note on <ref n="pl128.7">p. 128, l. 7</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlegae_p133_l1">:
							C. Antonius; see Introd. to Cat. 1, p.99. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_consilus_p133_l3">
							, <gloss>the public counsels</gloss>, i.e. his own (officially) as
							consul. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cam_abdicasset_p133_l4">
							, <gloss>after abdicating</gloss>. Lentulus could not properly be called
							to account during his magistracy; but he might be forced to resign, and
							could then be proceeded against. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erant_p133_l6">:
							notice that this and similar clauses in this section, since they are
							explanations made by Cicero and not parts of the decree, take the
							indicative. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Cassiam_p133_l7">,
							etc.: these last mentioned had not yet been arrested, but Ceparius was
							caught in his flight and brought back. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pastores_p133_l9">:
							Apulia was, as now, used chiefly for pasturage. In the summer, when
							these broad plains were dried up, the flocks were driven to the mountain
							pastures of Samnium and Lucania. These pastoral regions have always been
							the home of a lawless and restless population, prone to brigandage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="colonis_p133_l11">,
							etc.: Cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.20">20</ref>, above (pp.121,
							122). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sapplicatio_p133_l19">:
							a day of prayer, proclaimed by the Senate, either in thanksgiving (<foreign xml:lang="lat">gratulatio</foreign>) as in the present case, or in
							entreating favor of the gods. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eoram_p133_l20">,
							i.e. the gods. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="togato_p133_l21"> , <gloss>as
							a civilian</gloss>: cf. Cat. 1, <ref n="pl125.17">p. 125, l. 17</ref>
							and note. See Fig. 33. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liberassem_p133_l23">:
							in the decree, <foreign xml:lang="lat">liberavit</foreign>, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_interest_p133_l24">
							, <gloss>there
								is this difference</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bene_gesta_p133_l24">,
							as well as <foreign xml:lang="lat">conservata</foreign>, agrees with re <foreign xml:lang="lat">publica</foreign> (abl. abs.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faciendam_fuit_p133_l26">:
							observe that this form has not here its usual cont. to fact implication. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jas_p133_l29"> , <gloss>
							rights</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p133_l29">:
							he was allowed to resign instead of being put to death without resigning
							(as in the case below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qaae_faerat_p133_l30">
							, <gloss>what had not been a scruple to Afarius</gloss> a scruple which
							had not prevented M. from (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_minus_p133_l30">,
							etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qao_occideret_p133_l31">
							, <gloss>to prevent his killing</gloss>, following <foreign xml:lang="lat">religio</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 558">558</ref>, b
							(319, c); Cf. B. 295, 3; G. 549; H. 568, 8 (499, 3, N.1); H-B. 502, 31
							b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Glauciam_p133_l31">:
							see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl101.1">p. 101, l. 1</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nominatim_p133_l31">,
							i.e. Marius acted merely under the general authority conferred on him by
							the Senate in the formula, <foreign xml:lang="lat">Videatit
								consules</foreign>, etc. (see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl100.12">p.
							100, l. 12</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pellebam_p134_l5">:
							conative imperf.; cf. <ref n="pl127.16">p. 127, l. 16</ref> and note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pertimescendam_p134_l8">:
							observe the intensive force of <foreign xml:lang="lat">per</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_erat_p134_l9">,
							etc.: with this character of Catiline, cf. notes on Cat. 1, 26 (<ref n="pl109.23">p. 109, l. 23</ref>), and ii, 9 (p.117, t 4). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="continebatur_p134_l10">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 556">556</ref>, a (276, e, N.); G. 569; H.
							603 (519, i); H-B. 550, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consiliam_p134_l12">
							, <gloss>ability
								to plan</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mandarat_p134_l15">:
							for mood and tense, see § <ref n="AG 542">542</ref>, 518, b (322, 309,
							c); B. 287, 2; G. 567; H. 539, 2 (472, 2); H.-B. 577 and footnote I,
							579. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="depalissem_p134_l22">
							, <gloss>pushed
								aside</gloss>: the image is of averting a <gloss>crushing
								weight</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">molem</foreign>), just ready
							to fall. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_ille_p134_l23">,
							etc., i.e. as Cethegus did. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Saturnalia_p134_l23">,
							i.e. so distant a date. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="constituisset_p134_l23">:
							the prot. (cont. to fact) is implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_pablicae_p134_l24">:
							dat. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">denuntiavisset</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="testes_p134_l25">:
							in appos. with both </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="signum_p134_l25">
							and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="litterae_p134_l25">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qaae_p134_l26">:
							referring to Cicero's success in securing (lit. <gloss>capturing</gloss>)
							the evidence of guilt. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostis_p135_l3">
							(pred. appos.), <gloss>as an enemy</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cam_p135_l7">
							(correl. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_vera_p135_l7">,
							1.9), i.e. we cannot merely guess it (for the reason in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">quod</foreign>-clause following), but still more we
							can almost see it with our own eyes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_potuisse_p135_l8">
							(parenthetical), <gloss>because</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consili_p135_l8">
							(pred. gen. limiting <foreign xml:lang="lat">gubernatio</foreign>), <gloss>to
							belong to human wisdom</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possemas_p135_l11">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, a (287, a); B. 268, 7; G.
							511, a.3; H. 546 (495, i); H.-B. 481. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faces_p135_l12">,
							etc.: these omens are such as the Romans observed and noted carefully.
							Livy's history is full of them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetermittendam_p135_l16">,
							inadvertently; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relinquendam_p135_l16">,
							intentionally. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cotta_Torquato_p135_l18">:
							consuls <date when="-0065">B.C. 65</date>, the year in which Catiline
							first intended to carry out his conspiracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aera_p135_l21">:
							the laws were engraved on bronze tables. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_Romulas_p135_l22">:
							there is a bronze statue of the wolf suckling the infants in the
							Capitoline Museum at Rome, which bears marks either of lightning seaming
							one of its hind legs, or of some defect in the casting (Fig. 36). This
							is probably identical with that here mentioned. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haruspices_p135_l25">:
							see note on <ref n="pl130.14">p. 130, l. 14</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="flexissent_p135_l29">:
							in direct disc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">flexerint</foreign>, following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appropinquare_p135_l29">,
							which points to the future; § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d (307, d) ;
							G. 595; H. 580 (508, 4); cf. H.-B. 582, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illoram_p135_l29">,
							i.e. the <gloss>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">haruspices</foreign>
							</gloss>
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ladi_p135_l30">:
							festivals in which races and theatrical performances were celebrated in
							honor of the gods ; such festivals were especially appointed to appease
							the deities in times of danger and distress; Cf. Verres, 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.31">31</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_p136_l1">
							(plur.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">they also</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contra_atqae_p136_l3">
							, <gloss>opposite
								to what</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 324">324</ref>, c (156, a); B. 341,
							I, c; G. 643; H. 516, 3 (459, 2); H.-B. 307, 2, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_p136_l5">: the Forum
							and the Senate house (<foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign>) were east
							of the south end of the Capitoline Hill, on which stood the Capitolium,
							or temple of Jupiter Capitolinus (see Plan of Forum). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrarentur_p136_l7">:
							the word is chosen with reference to the omen of Jupiter looking toward
							the rising sun. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlocandam_locaverant_p136_l8">
							: <foreign xml:lang="lat">locare</foreign> with the gerundive is the
							regular expression for giving out a contract; § <ref n="AG 500">500</ref>,
							4 (294, d); B. 337, 7, b, 2; G. 430; H. 622 (544, N.2); H-B. 612, iii. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illi_p136_l9">,
							i.e. of year before last. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consulibas_nobis_p136_l10">:
							abl. abs. expressing the date. <milestone n="9" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeceps_p136_l13">
							, <gloss>
								headstrong</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mente_captus_p136_l13">
							, <gloss>
								insane</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_omnia_p136_l13">,
							i.e. the universe. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_comparari_p136_l15">,
							below. </gloss>
lemma: ita is explained by caedes . . . comparari

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="responsam_p136_l16">:
							the regular expression for any prophetic answer as of an oracle or seer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_pablicae_p136_l16">
							(dat.), <gloss>against the state</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="et_ea_p136_l17"> , <gloss>and
							that too</gloss> (cf. <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτα</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ea_p136_l18">:
							referring to <foreign xml:lang="lat">caedes</foreign>, etc, above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iliad_p136_l20">:
							referring forward to the result <foreign xml:lang="lat">at . . .
							statueretur</foreign> (ll.21-23).<pb n="400"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Concordine_p136_l22">:
							one of the principal temples at the northern end of the Forum (see
							Plan), where the Senate had held its session on this day. It was built
							by the consul L. Opimius, <date when="-0121">B.C. 121</date>, after his
							bloody victory over C. Gracchus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_p136_l26"> , <gloss>
							wherefore</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 414">414</ref>, a, N. (250, N.); H.-B.
							424, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vestris_p136_l28">,
							etc.: observe the contrast between <foreign xml:lang="lat">vestris</foreign>
							and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deorum_p136_l28">,
							which is emphasized by their respective positions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_ferendas_p136_l31">
							, <gloss>
								intolerable</gloss> for arrogance. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_p136_l31">,
							etc.: <foreign xml:lang="lat">anaphora</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 641">
								641</ref> (386); B. 350, II, b; cf. G. 682; H. 666, I (636, iii,
							3); H.-B. 632,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p137_l2">,
							etc.: omit the words in brackets as being a manifest gloss. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilium_p137_l6">,
							etc.: cf. the proverb, <gloss>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">quem</foreign> deus <foreign xml:lang="lat">
									perdere</foreign> volt, <foreign xml:lang="lat">prius</foreign>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">dementat</foreign>
							</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_neglegerent_p137_l7">, with which <foreign xml:lang="lat">id</foreign> is in apposition, the whole forming the
							subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">esse factum</foreign>. </gloss>
lemma:
at introduces the result clause at . . .
							neglegerent

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gens_whole_p137_l7">,
							not to the Allobroges in particular. </gloss>
lemma: gens refers here to the Gauls as a whole

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patricus_p137_l10">:
							the old patricians, though having no special political privileges, still
							retained considerable prestige as an hereditary aristocracy. cf. note on
							Verres 1.1 (<ref n="pl28.2">p. 28, l. 2</ref>). Of the conspirators,
							Catiline, Lentulus, and Cethegus were patricians. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_potuerint_p137_l12">:
							cf. note on <ref n="pl131.8">p. 131, l. 8</ref>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_p137_l12">, as
							subject of the charact. clause, may be translated by <gloss>when
								they</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pulvinaria_p137_l14">
							, <gloss>shrines</gloss>: properly <gloss>cushions</gloss>, upon which
							the statues of the gods were laid, when a feast was spread before them.
							This was called <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								lectisternium</foreign>, and was usually connected with the <foreign xml:lang="lat">supplicatio</foreign> (see note on <ref n="pl133.19">p. 133, l. 19</ref>). Only certain gods, chiefly
							Grecian, had <foreign xml:lang="lat">pulvinaria</foreign>, and the rite
							was established by direction of the Sibylline books (see note, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.9">9</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="celebratote_p137_l14">:
							the future imperative is used on account of its reference to a <gloss>set
							time</gloss> in the future; § <ref n="AG 449">449</ref> (269, d); G.
							268, ; H. 560, 4 (487, 11); H.-B. 496. The figure in the text (from an
							ancient altar relief) represents a procession such as was usual on
							occasions of this kind. Fig. 37 (from an ancient lamp) shows the images
							at such a feast. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duce_p137_l20">,
							i.e. in actual command; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperatore_p137_l20">,
							i.e. holding the sovereign power, whether actually commanding that
							particular operation or not. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dissensiones_p137_l22">:
							for case, see § <ref n="AG 350">350</ref>, d (219, b); G.376, a.2; H.
							455 (407, N.1); H.-B. 350, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Sulpicium_p137_l23">
							[<foreign xml:lang="lat">Rufum</foreign>], a young man of remarkable
							eloquence, a leader in the reforming party among the aristocracy. He was
							tribune <date when="-0088">B.C. 88</date>, and his quarrel with C.
							Caesar was the first act of the Civil War. By his proposition, the
							command in the Mithridatic War was transferred from Sulla to Marius; and
							when Sulla refused to obey, and marched upon the city, Sulpicius was one
							of the first victims. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlegam_p137_l27">:
							Lucius Cornelius Cinna, the Marian partisan (see note on <ref n="pl130.16">p. 130, l. 16</ref>). He and Cn. Octavius, a partisan
							of Sulla, were consuls <date when="-0087">B.C. 87</date>, after the
							departure of Sulla for the East, and in their dissensions the Civil War
							broke out afresh. The victory of Cinna later recalled Marius from exile. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lumina_p137_l29">:
							among these were Octavius; C. Caesar (see above) and his brother Lucius;
							Q. Catulus, father of the opponent of the Manilian Law (see below); M.
							Antonius, the great orator; and the <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex
								maximus</foreign>, Q. Scaevola. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ultus_est_p137_l30">:
							to preserve the emphasis, render <gloss>the cruelty</gloss>, etc., <gloss>was
							avenged by Sulla</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dissensit_p137_l32">
							, <gloss>there was a quarrel between</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Lepidus_p137_l32">,
							father of the triumvir, was consul <date when="-0078">B.C. 78</date>
							(after Sulla's death), with Q. Catulus, son of the one murdered by
							Cinna. The scheme of Lepidus to revive the Marian party resulted in a
							short civil war, in which he was defeated by his colleague and killed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsias_p138_l2">:
							he was the victim of his own violence, and therefore less regretted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p138_l3">:
							i.e. though these disturbances cost a great many lives, yet they were
							not so revolutionary as this conspiracy, which has been put down without
							bloodshed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commatandam_publicam_p138_l4">
							, <gloss>a change of government</gloss>
							<pb n="402"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quale_bellum_p138_l12">
							, <gloss>a war such as</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_bello_p138_l13">:
							§ <ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, a (200, a); B. 351, 4; G. 615; H.-B. 284,
							4. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnes_p138_l14">,
							etc., i.e. everybody except the desperate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantum_p138_l17"> , <gloss>only
							so many</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="restitisset_p138_l18">
							(<foreign xml:lang="lat">resisto</foreign>), <gloss>should
								survive</gloss> . <milestone n="11" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="matum_p139_l8">:
							such as a statue, for example. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eandem_diem_p139_l12">,
							etc., <gloss>the same period of time—eternal as I hope — is
								prolonged,
								both for the safety of the city</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duos_civis_p139_l15">,
							i.e. Pompey and himself. <milestone n="12" unit="chapter"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qaae_p139_l20"> , <gloss>
							as</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 308">308</ref>, h (201,g); H-B. 270, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="isti_p139_l22">
							(contrasted with <foreign xml:lang="lat">mihi</foreign>) refers to <foreign xml:lang="lat">illorum</foreign> (1.20). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mentes_p139_l24"> , <gloss>
							counsels</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_potest_p139_l27">
							, <gloss>no harm can be done</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dignitas_p139_l30">,
							etc., i.e. the majesty of the Roman state will be an invisible safeguard
							for me; cf. "the divinity "that" doth hedge a king" (<title>
								Hamlet</title> iv, 5, 123). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conscientiae_p139_l31">,
							etc., i.e. my enemies, conscious of their guilty sympathy with this
							conspiracy, will, in their attempts to injure me, inevitably commit some
							act which will show them to be traitors to the state. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="altro_p140_l2">,
							i.e. without waiting to be attacked. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domesticoram_hostiam_p140_l3">:
							oxymoron; § <ref n="AG 641">641</ref> (386); B. 375, 2; G. 694; H.
							752, 12 (637, xi, 6); H.-B. 632, 3. cf. the same figure in Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.21">21</ref> (<ref n="pl108.4">p. 108, l. 4</ref> ): <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum
								tacent, clamant</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convertit_p140_l4">:
							pres. for fut., as often, especially in <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								protasis</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obtulerint_p140_l6">:
							subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_vestro_p140_l8">:
							honor is used here, as usual, to denote external honors (offices)
							conferred by the people. Holding the consulship, he had nothing to look
							forward to. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conservanda_publica_p140_l12">:
							abl. of means. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_publica_p140_l14">
							, <gloss>in public life</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virtute_p140_l15">,
							non <foreign xml:lang="lat">casa</foreign>, etc., i.e. he will show this
							by such conduct as shall be consistent with this glorious achievement. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Jovem_p141_l1">:
							the temple of Jupiter Capitolinus is shown in the background of the
							illustration opposite p.136; the figure in the text at p.140 is a
							restoration of this temple. </gloss>
